title: Re-gluing a loose chair rail
subtitle: Clean the joint, apply hide glue, cramp and leave overnight
excerpt: How to re-glue a loose tenon joint in a chair rail: cleaning out the old dried glue, mixing and applying hide glue, reassembling the joint, and clamping until the glue sets.
sourceNotes: Traditional furniture joinery repair practice. John Makepeace Furniture Making (1995). English Heritage furniture conservation practice notes. Hide glue reversibility from BSRIA guidance on antique furniture repair.

content.text: Old chairs use
hide glue

throughout. The glue is water-soluble when warm, which is why joints work loose over time in centrally heated rooms. The same property makes repair straightforward: the old glue softens and cleans out cleanly, the joint goes back together with fresh hide glue, and the repair is as strong as the original.
Preparing the joint
Step 1, open the joint.

Ease the loose rail free from the mortise by hand or with gentle mallet taps. Forcing a stiff joint breaks fibres around the tenon shoulder; if the joint is still partially bonded, apply a few drops of warm water to the glue line and wait 5 minutes to soften the old glue before opening.
Step 2, clean the old glue.

Remove all dried hide glue from both the mortise walls and the tenon faces. Use a wooden tool or a stiff bristle brush dampened with methylated spirit to soften and lift the residue. The surfaces must be clean bare wood; fresh glue will not bond through old glue. Allow to dry fully before gluing.
Preparing the hide glue
Step 3, soak and warm the glue.

Place the pearl granules in a small container and cover with cold water. Soak for at least 30 minutes until the granules swell and absorb the water. Place the container in a pan of hot water (not boiling; 60 to 70 degrees Celsius is correct) and stir until the glue is smooth and fluid. It should run off a stick in a steady thin stream, not in clots. Keep the glue warm throughout the job; hide glue gels below 50 degrees and must not be applied cold.
Gluing and cramping
Step 4, apply the glue and assemble.

Brush warm hide glue onto all four faces of the tenon and into the mortise walls. Work quickly; hide glue has a short open time of about 5 minutes at room temperature. Drive the joint together with hand pressure or mallet taps, working squarely so the tenon enters the mortise without skewing.
Step 5, apply the sash cramp.

Position the sash cramp across the joint, applying pressure along the rail direction. Place a scrap of wood between the cramp jaw and the chair to protect the finish. Tighten the cramp until a small amount of glue squeezes out of the joint line; this confirms the glue is evenly distributed. Do not over-tighten; excess pressure starves the joint by squeezing out too much glue.
Step 6, check for square and leave overnight.

Check the chair sits flat on all four legs. If it rocks, apply a small amount of twist to the frame with gentle hand pressure while the glue is still workable. Wipe squeeze-out with a damp cloth before it sets. Leave the cramp in place overnight at room temperature before removing and testing the joint.
Common mistakes
Joint gels before it is fully home.

The glue was not warm enough, or the joint surfaces were too cold. Warm the tenon and mortise with a heat gun on low for 30 seconds before applying glue, and keep the glue at 60 to 70 degrees throughout the job.
Joint still loose after the glue has set.

Old glue was not fully removed, the joint was not cramped while the glue set, or PVA or modern adhesive was used over an existing hide-glue joint. PVA will not bond to old hide glue. Strip back to bare wood and re-glue with fresh hide glue.
